batch.name: Foundation Biology Batch 3 — Class 9 Tissues & Food Resources

questions.question: A group of cells that are similar in structure and work together to perform a particular function is called a:
questions.explanation: A tissue is a group of cells similar in structure and origin that work together to perform a specific function. This is the basic definition introduced in NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Meristematic tissue present at the tips of roots and shoots, responsible for increase in length, is:
questions.explanation: Apical meristem is present at the growing tips of stems and roots and increases the length of the plant. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: The meristem present at the base of leaves or internodes (e.g., in grasses) is known as:
questions.explanation: Intercalary meristem occurs at the base of leaves or at internodes and helps in increasing length. It is commonly seen in grasses. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of meristematic cells?
questions.explanation: Meristematic cells have very little or no vacuoles (no large vacuoles), dense cytoplasm, prominent nuclei and thin walls. Large vacuoles are a feature of permanent cells. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: The most common simple permanent tissue that forms the bulk of soft parts of a plant is:
questions.explanation: Parenchyma consists of relatively unspecialised living cells with thin walls and forms the bulk of the plant body, providing support and storing food. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Parenchyma containing chlorophyll and performing photosynthesis is called:
questions.explanation: Parenchyma with chlorophyll that performs photosynthesis is called chlorenchyma. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Large air cavities in parenchyma of aquatic plants that help them float are present in a tissue called:
questions.explanation: Aerenchyma is parenchyma with large air cavities that gives buoyancy to aquatic plants, helping them float. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Which tissue provides flexibility to plants and is commonly found in leaf stalks below the epidermis?
questions.explanation: Collenchyma provides mechanical support and flexibility, allowing easy bending of plant parts; it is found below the epidermis in leaf stalks and stems. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: The tissue that makes plant parts hard and stiff, such as the husk of a coconut, is:
questions.explanation: Sclerenchyma makes the plant hard and stiff; the husk of a coconut is made of sclerenchymatous tissue. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Which of the following sets of tissues are all simple permanent tissues?
questions.explanation: Simple permanent tissues are composed of one type of cell: parenchyma, collenchyma and sclerenchyma. Xylem and phloem are complex tissues made of more than one cell type. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: The tissue responsible for conduction of water and minerals from roots to leaves is:
questions.explanation: Xylem conducts water and minerals from the roots upward to other parts of the plant. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Which of the following components of xylem is the only living element?
questions.explanation: Xylem consists of tracheids, vessels, xylem parenchyma and xylem fibres. Among these, only xylem parenchyma is living; the rest are dead. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Which tissue transports food made in the leaves to other parts of the plant?
questions.explanation: Phloem transports prepared food (sugars) from leaves to the rest of the plant; this transport can be in both directions. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Phloem is made up of four types of elements. Which of the following is NOT a component of phloem?
questions.explanation: Phloem consists of sieve tubes, companion cells, phloem parenchyma and phloem fibres. Tracheids are a component of xylem, not phloem. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Among the components of phloem, which one is dead?
questions.explanation: In phloem, sieve tubes, companion cells and phloem parenchyma are living; only phloem fibres (bast fibres) are dead. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: In older woody stems, the cork (bark) cells are dead and their walls are thickened with a waterproof substance called:
questions.explanation: Cork cells are dead and contain suberin in their walls, making them impervious to gases and water. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: The type of epithelial tissue made up of a single layer of flat, thin cells found in the lining of blood vessels and alveoli is:
questions.explanation: Simple squamous epithelium is made of a single layer of flattened cells and lines blood vessels and alveoli of lungs, where transport and exchange occur. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: The epithelium that forms the lining of kidney tubules and ducts of glands, providing mechanical support, is:
questions.explanation: Cuboidal epithelium (cube-shaped cells) forms the lining of kidney tubules and ducts of glands, providing mechanical support. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: The tall, pillar-like epithelial cells that line the inner surface of the stomach and intestine for absorption and secretion form:
questions.explanation: Columnar epithelium consists of tall, pillar-like cells and lines the stomach and intestine where absorption and secretion occur. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Sometimes epithelial tissue folds inward and forms a multicellular gland. Such epithelium is referred to as:
questions.explanation: When epithelial cells fold inwards to secrete substances, they form glandular epithelium, e.g., the cells lining glands. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: The fluid (liquid) matrix of blood in which cells are suspended is called:
questions.explanation: In blood, the matrix is fluid and is called plasma. Red blood cells, white blood cells and platelets float in the plasma. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Which connective tissue connects muscle to bone and is strong but inflexible?
questions.explanation: Tendons connect muscles to bones; they have great strength but limited flexibility. Ligaments connect bone to bone. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: The connective tissue that connects two bones at a joint and is very elastic is the:
questions.explanation: Ligaments connect bone to bone; they are very elastic and have considerable strength, holding bones together at joints. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: The connective tissue with a solid yet flexible matrix that smoothens bone surfaces at joints and is found in the nose, ear and trachea is:
questions.explanation: Cartilage has a solid but flexible matrix and is present in the nose, ear, trachea and at the ends of bones, smoothening joint surfaces. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Fat-storing connective tissue found below the skin and between internal organs is:
questions.explanation: Adipose tissue is fat-storing connective tissue present below the skin and between internal organs; it acts as an insulator. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Bone is a connective tissue whose hard matrix is composed mainly of:
questions.explanation: Bone is a strong, non-flexible connective tissue with a hard matrix composed of calcium and phosphorus compounds (calcium salts) along with proteins. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Smooth muscles (unstriated muscles) are found in all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Smooth (unstriated, involuntary) muscles occur in the iris of the eye, ureters, bronchi and walls of internal organs like the bladder and alimentary canal. Limb muscles attached to bones are striated. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Which muscle tissue is involuntary, striated (shows light and dark bands) and shows rhythmic contraction throughout life?
questions.explanation: Cardiac muscle (heart muscle) is involuntary yet striated, and it contracts and relaxes rhythmically throughout life without tiring. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: The basic structural and functional unit of nervous tissue is the:
questions.explanation: Nervous tissue is made of cells called neurons (nerve cells), which are the structural and functional units that receive and conduct impulses. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: The single long part of a neuron that conducts the nerve impulse away from the cell body is the:
questions.explanation: A neuron has a cell body (cyton) with branched dendrites and a single long axon. The axon carries the impulse away from the cell body. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 6.

questions.question: Which of the following cereals is a source of carbohydrates in the diet?
questions.explanation: Cereals such as wheat, rice and maize provide carbohydrates for energy. Pulses (gram) give protein, and groundnut and mustard give oil/fat. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 15.

questions.question: Pulses such as gram, pea and moong are an important dietary source of:
questions.explanation: Pulses (legumes) such as gram, pea, moong and arhar are major sources of proteins in the diet. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 15.

questions.question: Plants require sixteen nutrients for growth. Which of the following pair of nutrients is obtained from air?
questions.explanation: Of the 16 essential nutrients, carbon and oxygen are supplied by air, hydrogen and oxygen also by water, and the remaining 13 by the soil. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 15.

questions.question: Nutrients that plants require in large quantities are called:
questions.explanation: Macronutrients are required by plants in large quantities (six of them), while micronutrients are needed in small quantities (seven of them). NCERT Class 9 Chapter 15.

questions.question: Of the thirteen nutrients supplied by the soil, the numbers of macronutrients and micronutrients are respectively:
questions.explanation: Of the 13 soil-supplied nutrients, 6 are macronutrients and 7 are micronutrients. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 15.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes manure from fertilizer?
options: Manure is rich in plant nutrients but poor in organic matter
Fertilizers add humus to soil while manure does not
Both manure and fertilizers are commercially produced inorganic salts
Manure adds humus and organic matter, while fertilizers supply specific nutrients
questions.explanation: Manure is prepared from decomposed plant and animal waste; it adds organic matter (humus) and improves soil structure. Fertilizers are commercially produced and supply specific nutrients (N, P, K) but do not add humus. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 15.

questions.question: Compost prepared by using earthworms to hasten decomposition of plant and animal refuse is called:
questions.explanation: When earthworms are used to speed up the process of decomposition of plant and animal refuse, the product is called vermicompost. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 15.

questions.question: Plants like sun hemp or guar grown and then ploughed back (mulched) into the soil to enrich it are an example of:
questions.explanation: Green manure is produced by growing certain plants (like sun hemp or guar) and then ploughing them into the soil, enriching it with nitrogen and phosphorus. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 15.

questions.question: Growing two or more crops simultaneously on the same field in a definite row pattern is called:
questions.explanation: Inter-cropping is growing two or more crops simultaneously on the same field in a definite pattern of rows, which improves yield and prevents pest spread. Mixed cropping has no set pattern. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 15.

questions.question: A desirable agronomic characteristic for fodder crops is:
questions.explanation: For fodder crops, tallness and profuse branching are desired characters, whereas for cereals dwarfness is preferred so plants need less nutrients. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 15.

questions.question: The rearing and management of honeybees for large-scale honey production is called:
questions.explanation: Apiculture is the rearing of honeybees (in beehives/apiaries) for honey and wax production. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 15.

questions.question: The practice of catching and rearing fish for food is known as:
questions.explanation: Pisciculture (fish farming) is the rearing and management of fish for food. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 15.

questions.question: Indian honeybee varieties used for commercial honey production include Apis cerana indica, Apis dorsata and Apis florea. Which Italian species is also widely reared for its high honey yield?
questions.explanation: Apis mellifera (the Italian bee) is an introduced variety reared for its high honey collection and gentle nature. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 15.

questions.question: In poultry farming, cross-breeding between indigenous (e.g., Aseel) and exotic (e.g., Leghorn) breeds develops new varieties. Which of the following is NOT a desired trait in such improvement?
options: Better quality and quantity of chicks/eggs
questions.explanation: Poultry breeding aims for heat tolerance, low maintenance, better egg/chick quality and resistance (not susceptibility) to disease. High disease susceptibility is undesirable. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 15.

questions.question: Supplying water to crops at appropriate intervals to ensure good growth is called:
questions.explanation: Irrigation is the supply of water to crops at suitable intervals; sources include wells, canals, rivers and tanks. NCERT Class 9 Chapter 15.
